question: What are the trade-offs between autonomous agents and workflow-based agents?

claim_lines: Autonomous agents optimize for **flexibility**: the model can choose its next step and tools at runtime. Workflow-based agents optimize for **control**: the path is predefined, so behavior is more predictable and easier to validate. Anthropic explicitly distinguishes these as different architectures, and recommends workflows for well-defined tasks and agents when flexible, model-driven decision-making is needed at scale. ([anthropic.com](https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents/))

- **Autonomous agents:** better on open-ended or changing tasks, but they usually add latency, cost, and operational risk because the system must reason, select tools, and recover from mistakes dynamically. ([anthropic.com](https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents/))

- **Workflow-based agents:** better for repeatable tasks, compliance, and debugging because the control flow is explicit; the downside is rigidity, and complex declarative flows can get cumbersome as branching increases. ([anthropic.com](https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents/))

- **Engineering effort:** workflow systems can be simpler to test and govern, while autonomous systems often require stronger guardrails, execution infrastructure, and careful tool design. ([openai.com](https://openai.com/business/guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-ai-agents/))

A practical rule from the sources is: **start with the simplest workflow that solves the problem, then add autonomy only when variability or scale justifies the extra complexity.** ([anthropic.com](https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents/))
